domain:
In the context of a theater organization, various plays are performed in one or more theaters, each identified by its own location, for example "The Northwest Theater". A play may have multiple scheduled sessions, and each session occurs on a scheduled date. Plays are identified by a unique name and is either a musical or an opera. Furthermore, each play may take place during the day or at night.

Each play is written by one or more authors, who are uniquely identified by name. Plays involve participants each also identified by name, and the same participant may act in multiple plays. For each play session, the starting time and an ending time is recorded, and participants can act in one or more sessions. Both authors and participant can be the same person, who may write a play and participate in its performance.

questions.question:
What is the minimum cardinality of Play in the relationship with Participant?

For example:
1. Minimum cardinality of zero, a participant can exist without any associated play.
2. Minimum cardinality of one, a participant must have at least one associated play.

Which option is better for you?
questions.option_1: Option 1: Zero as minimum cardinality, Participant can exist without Play.
questions.option_2: Option 2: One as minimum cardinality, Participant must have at least one Play.
questions.selected: Option 2: One as minimum cardinality, Participant must have at least one Play.

questions.question:
Should the relationship between Play and Session be modeled as a composition or as an association?

For example:
1. Use composition, Session would depend on Play for its existence.
2. Use association, Session could exist independently of Play, and can also be linked to multiple plays.

Which option is better for you?
questions.option_1: Option 1: Composition is needed because class Session depends on Play.
questions.option_2: Option 2: Association is sufficient because class Session exists independently of Play.
questions.selected: Option 1: Composition is needed because class Session depends on Play.

questions.question:
What is the minimum cardinality of Play in the relationship with Session?

For example:
1. Minimum cardinality of zero, a session can exist without any associated play.
2. Minimum cardinality of one, a session must have at least one associated play.

Which option is better for you?
questions.option_1: Option 1: Zero as minimum cardinality, Session can exist without Play.
questions.option_2: Option 2: One as minimum cardinality, Session must have at least one Play.
questions.selected: Option 2: One as minimum cardinality, Session must have at least one Play.

questions.question:
What is the minimum cardinality of Theater in the relationship with Session?

For example:
1. Minimum cardinality of zero, a session can exist without any associated theater.
2. Minimum cardinality of one, a session must have at least one associated theater.

Which option is better for you?
questions.option_1: Option 1: Zero as minimum cardinality, Session can exist without Theater.
questions.option_2: Option 2: One as minimum cardinality, Session must have at least one Theater.
questions.selected: Option 2: One as minimum cardinality, Session must have at least one Theater.

questions.question:
Should a generalization hierarchy be introduced between classes Participant and Author that share the following attributes: , and name?

For example:
1. Use the attributes , and name separated. The classes Participant and Author maintains independent attributes.
2. Use a common superclass Person containing the shared attributes , and name. This removes duplication in subclasses Participant and Author.

Which option is better for you?
questions.option_1: Option 1: Generalization is not needed, keep attributes (, and name) duplicated in each class.
questions.option_2: Option 2: Generalization is needed, create Person superclass with shared attributes, Participant and Author as subclasses.
questions.selected: Option 2: Generalization is needed, create Person superclass with shared attributes, Participant and Author as subclasses.

questions.question:
Should Session be modeled as an association class linking Theater and Play, or as a regular class with two separate associations?

For example:
1. Association class: Session exists only as part of the relationship between Theater and Play. A session cannot exist without the link between Theater and Play.
2. Regular class: Session is a class that has links to Theater and Play. In this case multiple sessions can exist for every Theater and Play.


Which option is better for you?
questions.option_1: Option 1: Association class, Session as association class linking Theater and Play.
questions.option_2: Option 2: Regular class, Session as separate class with two associations.
questions.selected: Option 2: Regular class, Session as separate class with two associations.

questions.question:
Should Person be modeled as an abstract class or as a concrete class?
Consider whether Person can be instantiated directly, or only through its subclasses (Participant or Author).

For example:
1. Concrete class Person that can be instantiated. Class people can exist independently.
2. Abstract class Person that cannot be instantiated. Every person must be a specific type participant or author.

Which option is better for you?
questions.option_1: Option 1: Concrete class Person that can be instantiated directly.
questions.option_2: Option 2: Abstract class Person, where only the subclasses can be instantiated.
questions.selected: Option 2: Abstract class Person, where only the subclasses can be instantiated.
